content:
6 min read
Just now

--

I was going to title this article: “Every Software Engineer should Vibe Code”, but that would have been a little bit too clickbaity. Although it’s true, there’s a lot of lessons to be learned from vibe coding.
As a supporter of my soccer team I decided to create a game that would show the geographical journey of a player and it’s up to the user to guess who the player is.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Map showing the journey of a players careerThe Prompt
I started by creating a new empty Angular project and then proceeded putting in the following prompt for the Pi Coding Harness using the Claude Sonnet 4.6 model.
Create a geographical guesser game in this current angular project for the supporters group of Austin FC.
Here's how the game works:
- We display a world map
- We display the geographical journey of a soccer player on the world map, connecting the various teams the player played for
- The end user has to guess the name of the soccer player out of a list of available names by typing in the name
- Hitting enter selects the soccer player
- If the wrong soccer player is selected, the user loses a life
- A player only has 3 lives
- The objective of the game is to correctly guess as many players as possible within a 2 minute window
- After 5 seconds of the player not guessing we display the names of the teams on the map
- After 30 seconds of the player not guessing we display the name of the player and the player loses a life
- We will create multiple levels of difficulty in the game that will manipulate the time and the number of lives so let's make those configurable in a json file
- Players will be stored in a json document as well with the name of the player, the jersey numbers they had at Austin FC and the different clubs they played at with the coordinates
- In the initial version create a list of 70 players and some coordinates of various soccer teams to start developing and testing the game
- Each location should have a dot, the longer the player was at a certain team the bigger the dotAs you can see, the prompt is a general outline of the game calling out the specific rules. Initially my thought was to focus on the game cause I knew the data would be tricky to get, once the game proves itself I would focus on getting the data populated.
Wild Goose Chase
Claude proceeded building the game but it also started researching the list of players for the game. It generated a list of players associated with the team but the team history it populated was incredibly inaccurate. That inaccurate list ended up wasting a lot of time, tokens and in the end money.
If I would have pointed out the wikipedia page to use as the source of truth it would have done way better. It still ended up needing corrections after that. I switched to the Gemini 3.1 Pro model for parsing the wiki pages in the end.
When you think you know better
The initial version of the world map was incredibly rudimentary. You could recognize the continents but it was very rough, so I decided to replace the map with a good looking world image instead.
Trying to get the application to put the dots in the correct location for a provided image ended up being impossible. The agent would try and debug the map, determine it was wrong, try again, and keep going. The earth is not flat but your screen is and this causes a lot of problems in cartography.
After several failed attempts, I ended up giving up on the image approach and I asked it to come up with a better solution or suggest which image I should use instead, and it ended up with a perfect solution using a well known visualization framework d3-geo.
Polishing
The final solution had a lot of gradients in the background, a lot of help text and a lot of emoticons. In general a lot of noise that didn’t improve the gameplay at all. It even made it worse because when text was rendered it would start shifting the layout of the map. I had to correct those issues and dial down the noise, as you can see in the search box example below.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Way too much instructional textAfter Polishing it looked like this
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Removed icons, gaps, and confusing and duplicate instructional textSculpting
The names of the locations were displayed on the map and while this was a great feature it ended up not working very well for the game. Often names would be overlapped and not readable so I decided to split those out in a separate table. This also helped the game in a mobile viewport.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Overlapping TextI decided to introduce difficulty levels because some players are not very well known. They’re on the roster but haven’t played a single minute. I decided to divide the players by sorting them by games played. Then take the top 50% for the easy category, the top 60% for the medium category, the top 80% for the hard category and all of the players for the expert category. This would raise the question, how many games played for each player within each category? As soon as I decided to show that on the dificulty screen, I realized it’s a lot easier to divide them by games played then by percentage. It also made the categories that much easier to understand to the end user.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Difficulty Selection ScreenBeing a player of the game myself, being curious, asking questions, was a big part of the development process. There’s a discovery of requirements and features during the process, that makes it a lot of fun.
Quality
The application had/has several quality issues.
The way the json files were getting loaded was not consistent. When adding the resources.json file, it put the file in a different folder and didn’t follow the same service architecture the other files used.
The global css file is not used at all currently which could ensure the same colors and styling are used throughout the application. Instead there are different styles in every component. This will need to be changed if we ever decide to repurpose this project for other sport teams or add components and we want the styling to be consistent.
The single unit test that existed was failing, the agent was able to fix it and create additional unit tests very easily.
The thing that impressed me was the fact that it used modern Angular syntax with input() and inject(). You would expect a lot of training data for Angular exists with the old syntax @Input(). This was due to the fact that the Angular generated AGENTS.MD file recommended it to do so.
Conclusion
During the development process, the following issues came up

• The agent is easily confused and goes off task

• It doesn’t always choose the correct or best solution

• It doesn’t always choose the same solution as the existing architecture

• It’s great at finding alternative solutions when asked

• The generated UI components needed polishing
Application development is iterative, especially in the beginning, they evolve while they’re being built, I don’t think it would have been possible for me to have a complete spec for this idea before starting the project.
This application can be taken further, we could standardize the styling and make it easy to repurpose for other teams. We could create a database back-end, track players scores and add other trivia. We could expand on the reporting aspect and add multiple visualization options as well.
To prevent the agent issues, we could introduce additional architectural instructions in the AGENTS.MD file for coding agents to follow.
We can add quality gates to check for violations we have identified.
This is not my first time coding this way, but I did learn some lessons. It was great getting introduced to the 3d-geo library as well.
In the end I will await some user feedback and see if there’s enough demand/interest to keep this project going.
